q: The two main aspects of the immune system are: Specificity
a: The ability to recognize self from non-self as well as distinguish pathogens and tumor cells from normal cells.

q: The two main aspects of the immune system are: Memory
a: The maintenance of specificity in a line of cells that remain in lymphoid tissue until the antigen that elicited their production reappears.

q: Know the definition of the following: Immunogen
a: A substance or antigen that can elicit an immune response.

q: Know the definition of the following: Antigen
a: A molecule or particle that is recognized by the immune system and can bind to specific antibodies or T-cell receptors.

q: Know the definition of the following: Antibody
a: A protein (immunoglobulin) produced by plasma cells in response to an antigen, which specifically binds to that antigen to neutralize or mark it for destruction.

q: Know the definition of the following: Interferon
a: Cytokines produced by virally infected cells that help protect neighboring uninfected cells from viral replication.

q: The Immune Response: Physical and chemical barriers (be able to list)
a: Physical: Skin, mucous membranes, ciliated epithelium. Chemical: Lysozyme in tears/saliva, stomach acid, and enzymes.

q: The actual immune response: Innate immunity
a: Present from birth, nonspecific. Membrane receptors have a broad specificity to mediate responses common to pathogens. Does not require prior exposure.

q: The actual immune response: Acquired (adaptive) immunity
a: Specific, requires prior exposure. Divided into cell-mediated and humoral immunity. Highly specific membrane receptors distinguish pathogens. Reacts rapidly with repeated exposure.

q: Architecture of the lymphatic system and lymph nodes
a: Consists of lymphatic vessels, lymph nodes, spleen, thymus, and tonsils. Lymph nodes act as filters where lymphocytes encounter antigens in the lymph.
Leukocytes including neutrophils, eosinophils, basophils, monocytes (macrophages), lymphocytes (T cells, B cells, NK cells), and dendritic cells.

q: Phagocytosis: Mononuclear phagocytes and granulocytic cells
a: Neutrophils, basophils, and eosinophils ingest solid material, including pathogens small enough to fit inside the cell.
Some pathogens can be phagocytized directly by the phagocytes using pattern recognition receptors that bind directly to pathogen-associated molecular patterns.
Capsulated bacteria have to be coated with antibody or complement factors in order to undergo phagocytosis.
Once ingested, the particle is in a phagosome, which fuses with a pre-existing lysosome, dumping hydrolytic enzymes to digest the particle.
The process where the phagocyte sticks to the wall of the blood vessel prior to exiting.
The process where phagocytes squeeze in between vascular endothelial cells to exit the bloodstream.
Directional migration to the site of infection, facilitated by inflammatory cytokines, complement proteins, and pathogen-associated molecules.
Natural Killer cells kill infected and tumor cells without prior sensitization.
A system of precursor proteins activated in cascade-fashion in response to tissue chemicals or pathogen-directed antibodies.
Inserted into the pathogen's membrane upon complement activation, resulting in cell swelling and lysis.
Activation results in peptides with roles in the immune system, such as opsonization (C3b) and chemotaxis (C5a).

q: Differences between active and passive immunity
a: Active immunity: The body produces its own antibodies and memory cells. Passive immunity: Pre-made antibodies are transferred from another source.

q: Which one would a flu shot be an example of?
a: Active immunity. The body is exposed to a harmless form of the antigen to stimulate its own antibody and memory cell production.

q: Process of clonal selection: Primary response
a: Slower response to immunogen. Antibodies of the M class predominate. B-cells proliferate into plasma cells and memory cells.

q: Process of clonal selection: Secondary response
a: Memory cells undergo clonal expansion. More rapid than primary response. Antibodies of the G class predominate.
Most abundant in serum, responsible for the secondary immune response.
Found primarily in mucosal areas and secretions such as saliva, tears, and breast milk.
The first antibody produced in the primary immune response.
Binds to mast cells and basophils, mediates allergic responses.
Functions primarily as an antigen receptor on the surface of naive B cells.

q: Structure of an antibody: Which part binds antigen?
a: The variable regions at the tips of the Y-shaped arms bind to the specific antigen.

q: Structure of an antibody: Which part binds to receptors on phagocytes?
a: The constant region (the stem of the Y) binds to receptors on phagocytes and activates complement.
Neutralization of pathogens/toxins, opsonization, agglutination, and activation of the complement system.

q: Differences and roles of T and B cells
a: T cells mediate cellular immunity against intracellular pathogens. B cells mediate humoral immunity against extracellular pathogens via antibodies.

q: Role of the thymus in the maturation of T lymphocytes
a: The site where immature T cells develop, differentiate, and undergo positive and negative selection for self-tolerance.

q: T cells vs B cell lineages (intracellular vs extracellular)
a: T cells defend against intracellular pathogens. Plasma cells (from B-cell lineage) secrete antibodies effective against extracellular antigens.

q: T cells insert cell receptors into their membranes
a: T cell receptors are placed on the surface to recognize and bind to specific foreign antigens presented by MHC molecules.
Found on all nucleated human cells. Recognized by cytotoxic T cells.
Found on macrophages, B-cells, and dendritic cells. Recognized by T-helper cells.

q: Process for the activation of T cells
a: Requires binding of the T-cell receptor to the antigen-MHC complex, plus a co-stimulatory signal from the antigen-presenting cell.
Destroy infected host cells and tumor cells directly.
Secrete cytokines to stimulate B cells to make antibodies and to activate cytotoxic T cells and macrophages.

q: Aspects of the immune response against extracellular bacteria
a: Involves the inflammatory response, complement activation, phagocytosis, and B cell activation for antibody production.

q: Aspects of the immune response against viruses
a: Involves interferon production, NK cell activation, antibody neutralization, and cytotoxic T cell destruction of infected cells.
An immune response to a nonpathogenic antigen.
A rapid allergic reaction mediated by antibodies.
An allergic reaction that takes days to develop and is mediated by helper T cells.
A severe, systemic, life-threatening immediate hypersensitivity reaction.

q: Allergic response mediated by IgE: First exposure
a: Elicited on first exposure to the allergen, after which the titer of IgE builds up over time.

q: Allergic response mediated by IgE: Second exposure
a: The complex of IgE and the antigen bind to the surface of mast cells (tissue basophils).
Following IgE binding, mast cells degranulate, producing the mediator chemicals of the allergic response.

q: Role of MHC in antigen presentation
a: MHC proteins act as display molecules on cell surfaces, presenting peptide fragments to T cells for recognition.

q: Role of MHC in recognition of self vs. non-self
a: MHC proteins identify cells as self; variations trigger rejection of non-self tissue. Designated as HLA in humans.
Makes up A, B, AB, or O groups based on red blood cell surface antigens, and Rh positive or negative.
Type O-negative, as its red blood cells lack A, B, and Rh antigens, preventing an immune response in the recipient.
Type AB-positive, as the plasma lacks antibodies against A, B, or Rh antigens.
Infusing the wrong blood group causes recipient antibodies to agglutinate and lyse the donor RBCs.
The immune system's failure to respond to an individual's own antigens.
The body develops autoimmune diseases.
Examples include Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus, Multiple Sclerosis, Rheumatoid Arthritis, Systemic Lupus Erythematosus.
The ongoing monitoring process by which the immune system actively identifies and destroys abnormal cells, such as tumor cells.
Refers to brain-immune system interactions.
Involves an interaction between the Brain, Endocrine, and Immune systems.
Cytokines, neuropeptides, and hormones are the effectors of this interaction.
A fast reaction to stress mediated by the sympathetic nervous system.
Levels of cortisol spike during stress, exerting an immunosuppressive effect on the immune system.
Your immune system is less capable of fighting disease under stress due to immunosuppressive cortisol spikes.

q: Muscle metabolism: Aerobic sources of ATP
a: Requires oxygen. Produces a high yield of ATP primarily through oxidative phosphorylation.

q: Muscle metabolism: Anaerobic sources of ATP
a: Does not require oxygen. Produces ATP rapidly but in low yields via glycolysis, resulting in lactic acid.

q: Role of lactate and fatty acids in gluconeogenesis and oxidative phosphorylation
a: Lactate is converted to glucose in the liver. Fatty acids are broken down to fuel oxidative phosphorylation.

q: Role of liver and muscle glycogen
a: Muscle glycogen provides localized glucose for exercise. Liver glycogen is broken down to maintain adequate blood glucose levels.
Promotes gluconeogenesis, lipolysis, and protein breakdown to provide metabolic substrates.
Epinephrine and norepinephrine stimulate glycogenolysis and lipolysis, increasing fuel availability.

q: Hormonal regulation during exercise: Growth hormone
a: Promotes lipolysis and gluconeogenesis, preserving blood glucose for the brain.
Stimulates hepatic glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis to raise blood glucose levels.

q: Why is insulin secretion SUPPRESSED during exercise?
a: To prevent active muscles from rapidly depleting blood glucose and to allow hepatic glucose release.

q: How can muscle cells utilize glucose effectively with insulin suppression?
a: Muscle contractions independently stimulate the translocation of GLUT4 transporters to the cell membrane, allowing glucose uptake.
Ventilation rate increases right at the inception of exercise before changes in O2/CO2 partial pressures, anticipating metabolic demand.
Rises sharply and shifts the distribution of blood toward muscle primarily.
Peripheral resistance falls (vasodilation) to accomplish the blood flow shift to the muscles.
Goes UP slightly because the resulting increase in cardiac output overcomes the vasodilation-induced fall in resistance.
Increased heat is a consequence of increased metabolism.
Induces dehydration and lowered blood volume.

q: Redirecting blood flow to the skin issues
a: Induces further vasodilation, leaving less blood to muscles. The body redirects effort away from heat control to keep brain blood flow optimal, causing overheating.
Lowers the risk of type 2 diabetes mellitus due to improved glucose uptake.
Improves immune function, as long as exercise is not overly strenuous.
Embryo has the potential to develop either male or female organs.
Mullerian Duct, Wolffian Ducts, and Bipotential gonads.

q: Sexual development with testosterone: SRY gene
a: Directs male development; the gonadal cortex regresses and the gonadal medulla forms the testis.

q: Sexual development with testosterone: Wolffian Duct
a: Forms the epididymis, vas deferens, and seminal vesicle.

q: Sexual development with testosterone: Leydig cells
a: Located in the testes, secrete testosterone.

q: Sexual development with testosterone: Sertoli cells
a: Located in the testes, secrete AMH which induces the regression of the Mullerian Duct.
Gonadal cortex forms the ovary, while the gonadal medulla regresses.
Wolffian Duct regresses. Mullerian duct becomes the fallopian tube, uterus, cervix, and upper vagina.
Plays a central role in the production of progesterone, testosterone, estradiol, and DHT.
Converts testosterone into estradiol; plays a key role in female sex hormone development.
Primary gametocytes undergo meiosis I. Secondary gametocytes undergo meiosis II.
Spermatogonia/Oogonia and Primary gametocytes are diploid. Secondary gametocytes and mature gametes are haploid.
Primary gametocytes have double their normal content of DNA before dividing, though they are still considered diploid.
Males: Continuous from puberty. Females: Mitosis in utero, meiosis pauses until puberty, then pauses again until fertilization.
Secreted from the hypothalamus to stimulate the anterior pituitary.
Secreted into the blood by the anterior pituitary in both sexes.

q: Gonadotropin axis: LH stimulation and feedback
a: Stimulates endocrine cells of the gonads to secrete hormones that feedback inhibit the pituitary and hypothalamus.
Stimulates gamete production.
Seminal vesicles, prostate gland, and bulbourethral glands contribute to the formation of semen.
Occurs within the seminiferous tubules of the testis.
Support sperm production.
Secrete testosterone in the interstitial space.

q: Male hormonal axis: Sertoli cell secretions
a: Secrete Androgen-binding protein (ABP) to keep testosterone localized, and inhibin to feedback-inhibit FSH production.
Surround the developing oocyte, secrete estrogen and inhibin, and nourish the oocyte.
Outer layer of the follicle, produce androgens which are converted to estrogen by granulosa cells.
Days 1-14. Follicles grow and mature, driven by FSH, secreting increasing levels of estrogen.
Triggered around day 14 by a massive LH surge.
Post-ovulation. The ruptured follicle becomes the corpus luteum, secreting progesterone.
Maintains the uterine wall when there is a pregnancy, during the time before the placenta takes over.

q: Human sexual response: Erectile tissue physiology
a: Vasodilation fills erectile tissue with blood. The parasympathetic division of the autonomic nervous system predominates.
Ranges from abstinence to hormonal (pills, IUDs), barrier methods (condoms), and behavioral methods.
The final maturation step of sperm occurring in the female reproductive tract, allowing them to swim rapidly and fertilize an egg.
In the distal portion (ampulla) of the Fallopian tube.
Enzymes in the sperm head break down the oocyte's zona pellucida, allowing the spermatocyte to penetrate.
Chemical changes in the oocyte membrane block other sperm from entering, preventing polyspermy.

q: Completion of meiosis in the oocyte
a: The secondary oocyte is paused at metaphase II and only completes the process of meiosis AT fertilization.
Elevated levels of Human Chorionic Gonadotropin (HCG) are detectable by the mother in a urine test shortly after implantation.
The zygote implants in the wall of the uterus roughly a week after fertilization.
Nourishes the fetus, provides oxygen exchange, and supplies the hormones necessary to maintain the uterine endometrium.
The relationship between uterine stretch by the growing fetus initiates signals that begin labor.
Labor is one of the examples of positive feedback in the body, driven by stretch, oxytocin, and contractions.
Prolactin stimulates milk production by the mammary glands.
Oxytocin stimulates smooth muscle contraction around the nipple of the breast.
END
